html:
Intermap is a small geospatial data company that is still priced like a volatile project contractor, but the forward setup is closer to a cash-rich, underfollowed data-rights company with a live shot at very large government conversion.

The headline screen is ugly. Q1 2026 revenue was only $1.4 million, down from $4.3 million, and adjusted EBITDA was negative $2.1 million. But the latest Q1 filing shows why the screen is misleading: acquisition-services revenue was zero because Indonesia follow-on contracting slipped, while software and solutions still did $1.2 million and recurring subscription/data revenue was more than 80% of total revenue. The bad quarter exposed how small the project revenue was without government milestone recognition, but it also exposed the new revenue mix.

The company’s real asset is NEXTMap, a proprietary 3D elevation and geospatial-intelligence database built with radar, LiDAR, photogrammetry, satellite, and derived analytics. This is not generic GIS consulting. High-quality elevation data becomes embedded in underwriting, flood-risk scoring, defense planning, UAV routing, disaster modeling, and sovereign mapping workflows. Once an insurer or agency builds models around a terrain dataset, switching is not just a vendor swap. It means revalidating models, re-underwriting portfolios, and accepting operational risk.

The market’s variant perception is that Intermap missed 2025 guidance and then missed again optically in Q1. The better read is that 2025 and Q1 2026 were timing casualties while the commercial data business kept compounding. In 2025, subscription and data revenue grew 29% to $5.2 million and became 49% of total revenue, while commercial recurring or repeating revenue rose to 60% of total revenue from 32% in 2024, per the FY2025 release. That is the part of the business that should carry software/data multiples, not trailing contractor multiples.

The upside torque is unusually high because the government pipeline is oversized relative to the equity. Intermap says it has been down-selected for all four remaining lots of Indonesia’s ILASP program, representing a potential $200 million opportunity. It is also pursuing U.S. federal GEOINT opportunities, including NGA-linked Luno programs. None of this should be capitalized at full value today, but it matters that a company with roughly a $85-100 million public equity value is competing for individual programs that can equal or exceed its market cap.

The commercial side is becoming more credible at the same time. In April, eight leading Czech insurers, representing a majority of the Czech residential property insurance market, adopted Intermap’s AI flood-risk platform for nationwide underwriting and portfolio risk management. In Malaysia, Intermap was awarded work tied to flood forecasting and national resilience, while the company reiterated that insurers are using its elevation data for catastrophe modeling and underwriting. These are small disclosures financially, but they point to the right kind of demand: recurring, data-led, and embedded in regulated risk workflows.

Valuation is the reason to care now. Management reaffirmed 2026 guidance of $30-35 million of revenue and a 28% EBITDA margin in the Q1 release. At the midpoint, that is about $9 million of EBITDA. Against an enterprise value around $70-85 million depending on venue, FX, and cash treatment, the stock trades around 8-9x forward EBITDA if guidance is even roughly right. That multiple is not demanding for a business with a growing data subscription base, net cash, and government contract optionality.

The balance sheet is no longer the immediate bear case. Intermap ended Q1 with $18.8 million of cash and $16.3 million of working capital. Shares outstanding were 73.8 million on May 13, and remaining warrants are modest. The company did raise equity in 2025, including a large bought deal, so dilution risk is not theoretical. But the current cash position gives management time to convert delayed awards without an emergency financing.

The key risk is simple: the guidance is back-end loaded and contract-dependent. Q1 revenue of $1.4 million means Intermap needs a sharp revenue ramp over the final three quarters of 2026 to hit $30-35 million. If Indonesia or U.S. federal work slips again, the market will treat the guidance as aspirational. The other risk is disclosure quality. Intermap still does not give investors clean ARR, backlog conversion, or customer concentration detail. For a company asking the market to revalue it as a data platform, that is a real gap.

Retail context is favorable but thin. This is not a crowded meme stock. Discussion is mostly small-cap investors debating Indonesia procurement details, while broader Reddit and StockTwits attention appears minimal. The lack of institutional ownership and wide spreads make the stock fragile, but they also leave room for a violent re-rating if one large government award or clean recurring-revenue disclosure lands.

Using the latest TSX close of CAD 1.85 from YCharts, translated at roughly 0.625 CAD/EUR, the current Frankfurt-equivalent price is about €1.16.

Bear case: €0.60, down 48%. Indonesia slips into 2027 or is lost, U.S. awards remain task-order noise, subscription growth is too small to offset burn, and the shelf overhang forces another discounted raise.

Base case: €2.25, up 94%. Intermap comes near 2026 guidance, recurring data revenue keeps growing, one meaningful government program converts, and the market values the company at roughly 12-14x forward EBITDA plus cash and database optionality.

Bull case: €4.75, up 309%. Indonesia converts in size, U.S. GEOINT task orders follow, European insurance adoption broadens, revenue scales toward $45-60 million with high incremental margins, and Intermap is valued as a strategic geospatial data asset rather than a small contractor.
